skill.name: changelog-automation
skill.description: Automate changelog generation from commits, pull requests, and releases using Keep a Changelog and Conventional Commits practices.
skill.summary: Generate consistent changelogs and release notes from repository history.
skill.author: wshobson

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command, network, token, and weak-crypto patterns. Review found no malicious code or prompt injection; most flags are documentation examples, Markdown fences, URLs, and false positives from words such as description. The skill is publishable with warnings because it teaches release workflows that can push tags, create releases, and use GitHub or npm tokens.

medium_findings.title: Release Automation Examples Use Write Tokens
medium_findings.description: The workflow example grants repository write permissions and uses GitHub and npm tokens. This is legitimate release automation, but copying it without review can publish packages or push release changes.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines clearly show write permissions and token-backed release steps. The context is instructional, so the risk is operational rather than malicious.

medium_findings.title: Examples Include Commands That Modify Project State
medium_findings.description: The skill provides commands for installing tools, writing configuration, bumping versions, generating changelogs, and pushing tags. These commands are expected for the skill, but should not be run without user confirmation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible in fenced examples and directly affect repository files or release state. They appear legitimate for changelog automation.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Documentation References
low_findings.description: The detected URLs point to standards, example GitHub compare links, and project resources. No evidence found that the skill sends data to these URLs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are Markdown links or template examples, not executable network calls. Their context matches public changelog documentation.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged text such as description and changelog-related examples. No evidence found of cryptographic APIs, hashing code, or security-sensitive algorithm selection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are prose, placeholders, or configuration values. They do not implement encryption, hashing, or authentication logic.

dangerous_patterns.title: Static Code Execution Heuristic Dismissed
dangerous_patterns.description: The dangerous-combination heuristic is not confirmed. The skill is a Markdown guide with examples, and no executable skill script or exfiltration path was found.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file contains command examples, public links, and token placeholders in release workflow templates. No evidence found of hidden execution or data exfiltration.

content.value_statement: Manual changelogs are slow and inconsistent across teams. This skill helps configure release notes, commit rules, and version workflows from repository history.
actual_capabilities: Explains Keep a Changelog structure and release section conventions.
Maps Conventional Commit types to changelog categories.
Provides setup patterns for commitlint, Husky, standard-version, and semantic-release.
Shows GitHub Actions release workflows for automated changelog generation.
Includes templates for public and internal release notes.
Covers git-cliff and commitizen configuration examples.
limitations: It is a documentation skill, not a standalone changelog generator.
Release commands can modify files, tags, packages, and repository releases.
Examples require adaptation for each repository, package manager, and release policy.
Token-based publishing workflows need human review and protected secrets.

use_cases.title: Standardize Team Commit Messages
use_cases.description: Define Conventional Commit rules and validation so future changelogs can be generated reliably.

use_cases.title: Prepare Automated Releases
use_cases.description: Set up semantic-release or standard-version workflows for version bumps and release notes.

use_cases.title: Create Clear Release Notes
use_cases.description: Turn pull requests and commits into readable notes for users, support teams, and internal stakeholders.

prompt_templates.title: Draft a Changelog Format
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for an existing repository.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Keep a Changelog structure for this project. Use sections for Added, Changed, Deprecated, Removed, Fixed, and Security.

prompt_templates.title: Map Commits to Sections
prompt_templates.scenario: Convert repository history into release categories.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these Conventional Commit messages and group them into changelog sections. Flag any unclear or invalid commit messages.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Release Workflow
prompt_templates.scenario: Plan automation before editing CI files.
prompt_templates.prompt: Recommend a release automation approach for this repository. Compare standard-version, semantic-release, git-cliff, and commitizen.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Release Automation
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this changelog automation workflow for safety, token scope, branch protection, versioning behavior, and rollback risks.

output_examples.input: A repository has feature, fix, and performance commits since the last tag.
output_examples.output: A changelog draft with separate Added, Fixed, and Changed sections, plus a suggested minor version bump.

output_examples.input: A team wants automated npm releases from the main branch.
output_examples.output: A release plan covering commit rules, branch protections, token requirements, dry runs, and publishing approval steps.

output_examples.input: A product manager needs customer-facing release notes.
output_examples.output: A concise release summary with highlights, breaking changes, known issues, and upgrade guidance.

best_practices: Use dry runs before enabling automated publishing.
Keep release tokens scoped and stored in protected secret managers.
Require clear Conventional Commit rules before generating changelogs automatically.
anti_patterns: Do not run release commands without confirming repository state and target branch.
Do not grant broad package or repository permissions when narrower scopes are available.
Do not publish generated changelogs without checking breaking changes and security notes.

faq.question: Does this skill generate changelogs by itself?
faq.answer: No. It provides patterns, templates, and setup guidance for changelog tools and release workflows.

faq.question: Which changelog format does it prefer?
faq.answer: It centers on Keep a Changelog and maps Conventional Commits into common release sections.

faq.question: Can it help with GitHub Actions releases?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes workflow examples for semantic-release, standard-version, and GitHub release notes.

faq.question: Is it safe to run the included commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. Review commands first because release automation can change files, push tags, and publish packages.

faq.question: Does it support Python projects?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes commitizen guidance for Python projects using pyproject configuration.

faq.question: What security review is needed before publishing?
faq.answer: Check token scopes, branch protection, dry-run behavior, package publishing settings, and rollback plans.
